narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer despite the challenges many face in this field. Your background includes a high family income bracket, which is a positive factor, along with planning to attend full-time like most students, an undergraduate GPA of 2.80, an LSAT score of 35, and school deciles of 2 in Year 1 and 3 in Year 3 based on your grades. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 71.8% chance that you would fail it, leading to the rejection of your application to protect the school's outcomes. This prediction was driven primarily by your lower Year 1 decile ranking, which stood out as the strongest factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure, well below the typical student's mid-range decile of around 5-6. Your Year 3 decile, also on the lower side compared to averages, reinforced this concern as the second most influential element, suggesting inconsistent academic performance over time. Your undergraduate GPA, slightly below the average of 3.21, added a modest push in the same direction, while your LSAT score of 35, though respectable, was a bit under the typical 36.7 and contributed similarly. On a brighter note, your high family income was the key factor pulling the prediction toward success, counteracting some risks. Being full-time, like over 90% of students, had almost no impact. Compared to typical applicants who pass the bar prediction, your early academic deciles were notably weaker, tipping the scales despite your strengths. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar starts through targeted improvement. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT or strengthening your GPA with additional coursework, perhaps seeking mentorship programs tailored for Black women in law to boost your profile for future applications. You're capable of turning this around; keep pushing.
